entries.title: Sessions: overview of session handling, cookie jar, macros
entries.body:
# Sessions - session management in Burp

The **Settings > Sessions** section solves one big automation pain point: how to make Burp **keep you logged in by itself** during scans, Intruder attacks, and crawling. Without this mechanism any long scan sooner or later drops out on the session: the token expired, the cookie changed, the CSRF token went stale - and from then on Burp keeps hammering endpoints while logged out, collecting garbage instead of bugs.

Inside there are three related blocks, and they work together:

- **Session handling rules** - rules that Burp applies to EVERY outgoing request (within its scope). This is exactly where the auto-login logic is described: "before the request, insert cookies from the jar", "check whether the session is alive, and if not - run the login macro".
- **Cookie jar** - a shared cookie jar where Burp stores all cookies issued by sites. Rules can mix cookies from the jar into requests.
- **Macros** - a recorded sequence of one or more requests (for example, a login flow). Macros are invoked from rules.

> All three blocks are **project-level settings**. They travel with the project file, not with the global settings. Move the project - the session rules move with it.

## Why this matters in practice

A classic scenario: you launch an **active audit** against an application with authentication. After 15 minutes the server session expires by timeout. Without session handling, Scanner will keep sending requests with a dead cookie and find nothing behind the authentication wall. With the right rule, Burp notices the logout and automatically re-logs in via a macro - the scan continues with a valid session.

## Cookie jar

**Open cookie jar** opens the cookie table: name, domain, path, value. You can edit it by hand, delete individual cookies, or clear everything entirely. In the jar settings you mark which tools' traffic it monitors (where it picks cookies up from):

```
Monitor cookies from:
  [x] Proxy
  [x] Scanner
  [x] Repeater
  [ ] Intruder
  [ ] Sequencer
  [ ] Extensions
```

> Subtlety: **crawling (crawl) does NOT update the cookie jar** and applies session handling rules almost not at all - the exceptions being the "Set a specific cookie or parameter value" and "Invoke a Burp extension" actions. That is why auto-login via a macro works fully at the **audit** stage, not at the pure crawl stage.

## How this shows up in the tools

- The **default rule** already, out of the box, mixes cookies from the jar into Scanner requests.
- In **Repeater** and **Intruder** you see the FINAL request - already after the rules are applied (with the cookies/parameters substituted in).
- Scanner reports show the ORIGINAL request (before the rules). To see what actually went out to the server, send the issue to **Repeater**.
- When **fuzzing** a specific parameter in Intruder, rules that touch that very parameter are not applied - otherwise Burp would overwrite your payload.

## Debugging

The main diagnostic tool is **Open sessions tracer**. It shows, step by step, what Burp did with the request: which rule fired, which macro ran, which cookies/parameters were substituted. If auto-login "does not work" - go here first: you can see at which step everything broke.

> Pitfall: session handling is the most finicky part of Burp. 90% of "the scan finds nothing behind authentication" problems are cured via the tracer: usually either the macro fails to pull a fresh CSRF token, or the session validity check rule is set to the wrong logout indicator.

entries.title: Session handling rules: scope and actions (auto-login during scans)
entries.body:
# The session handling rule editor

A rule is "what to do with the request" + "which requests to apply this to". It opens via **Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add** (or **Edit** on an existing one). The editor window consists of two tabs: **Details** (description + list of actions) and **Scope** (the area of application).

Burp runs the rules for each outgoing request and performs all matching actions **in order from top to bottom**, until some action says "apply no further".

## The Scope tab - where the rule hits

Three dimensions of scope:

- **Tools scope** - which tools are involved (Proxy, Scanner, Repeater, Intruder, Sequencer, Extensions). For auto-login during scans, be sure to enable **Scanner**.
- **URL scope** - all URLs / suite scope (the overall Target scope) / your own list by URL match. It is practical to tie it to **suite scope**, so the rule does not reach out to extraneous hosts.
- **Parameter scope** - restrict the rule to requests that contain a specific named parameter. Handy for targeted work with a CSRF token.

```
Scope:
  Tools:  [x] Scanner  [x] Repeater  [x] Intruder  [ ] Proxy ...
  URL:    Use suite scope
  Params: (optional) only requests with the csrf_token parameter
```

## The Details tab - actions (rule actions)

This is the core. You hit **Add** and choose an action. Available:

- **Use cookies from the session handling cookie jar** - insert cookies from the shared jar into the request (all of them or only the specified ones). The basic building block: "go with up-to-date cookies".
- **Set a specific cookie or parameter value** - set a value for a named cookie/parameter/header; if absent - add it (as a URL parameter, body parameter, or cookie). One of the few actions that works even during a crawl.
- **Check session is valid** - make a test request and, from the response, figure out whether the session is alive. Based on the result - conditional sub-actions (for example, "if logged out -> run the login macro"). This is the brain of auto-login.
- **Prompt for in-browser session recovery** - ask you to recover the session manually via Burp's browser (when there is no way to do it automatically - CAPTCHA, 2FA).
- **Run a macro** - run a recorded macro (usually a login flow) to obtain a fresh valid session; after the macro you can update cookies/parameters in the current request from the macro's last response.
- **Run a post-request macro** - first send the CURRENT request, then run another macro (for example, to carry a multi-step process through to the end).
- **Invoke a Burp extension** - hand session handling over to an extension (custom logic on the Montoya API). Also works during a crawl.
- **Set a specific header value** - set/add a header (for example, a fresh `Authorization: Bearer ...`).
- **Replace matching request content** - a crude replacement by string/regex anywhere in the request.

## A recipe for auto-login during an active scan

The goal is for Scanner to re-log in by itself when the session goes stale. We assemble the rule from a chain of actions:

```
Rule actions (order matters):
  1. Check session is valid
       Request to issue:  GET /account  (a page accessible only when logged in)
       Session valid if:  response matches  "Logout"   (a logout button present = we are inside)
       If session is invalid -> run sub-actions:
          a. Run macro:  [Login]         <- log in again
          b. (the macro will update the session cookie and a fresh CSRF in the jar)
  2. Use cookies from the cookie jar    <- insert the up-to-date cookies into the current request

Scope:
  Tools:  Scanner (+ Repeater for manual checking)
  URL:    suite scope
```

The logic: before each (or periodically before a) scan request, Burp pings `/account`. It sees "Logout" - the session is alive, we move on. It does not see it - that means we are logged out, we run the `[Login]` macro, it puts fresh cookies/token into the jar, and the current request goes out already authenticated. The scan does not fall apart over a long distance.

> Practice: choose a reliable and stable session validity indicator ("Session valid if") - better a unique string from a private area (a username, "Sign out") than a response code: many applications on logout return a `200` with a redirect to login, and a status-based check will deceive you.

> Pitfall: if the login macro itself pulls a CSRF token from the login page, do not forget to configure that in the macro itself (derive parameter / custom parameter location) - otherwise the repeated login will fail on a stale token. All of this is visible in the **sessions tracer**.

entries.title: Macros: recording and configuring login sequences
entries.body:
# Macros - recorded sequences of requests

A macro is a pre-recorded chain of one or more HTTP requests that Burp can play back automatically. The main use in hunting is a **login macro for auto-login**: a session handling rule sees that the session has gone stale and runs the macro, which goes through login again and obtains fresh cookies/tokens.

It is configured in **Settings > Sessions > Macros**. **Add** opens the **Macro Recorder**.

## Recording a macro

1. **Add** -> the Macro Recorder opens with a list of requests from Proxy history.
2. Select the needed requests (for example: `GET /login` -> `POST /login` -> `GET /account`). You can add new ones by walking the site through Burp's browser right from the recorder.
3. **OK** - the selected items land in the **Macro Editor**.

> Important: you cannot record new requests through the browser while intercept is on. The recorder has an **Intercept is on** button - turn it off before recording.

## Macro Editor - fine-tuning the macro

In the editor there is an editable list of macro items. You can:

- select a request and tweak it right in the viewer;
- **Configure item** - open the configuration of the selected item (cookies and parameters);
- **Re-analyze macro** - re-run the auto-detection of relationships between requests;
- **Test macro** - play the macro and look at the result.

## Configure item - the most important thing for CSRF

For each request in the macro you configure:

**Cookie handling:**
- whether to add received cookies to the shared cookie jar;
- whether to mix cookies from the jar into the outgoing request.

**Parameter handling** - three modes per parameter:

- **Use a preset value** - a fixed value (for example, a username/password).
- **Derive from prior response** - take the value from the RESPONSE of a prior request in the macro, by parameter name and URL. When creating a macro Burp itself tries to find such relationships: it looks for matching form fields, query strings, redirect targets. This automatically solves the problem of CSRF tokens that sit in ordinary form fields.
- **Custom parameter locations in response** - if the token sits NOT in a standard place (for example, embedded in JS, in JSON, in a header), you define the extraction manually: **Add** -> parameter name -> whether URL encoding is needed -> the extraction rule (see **Response extraction rules**: "start after / end at" or a regex group).

```
A typical login macro with CSRF:
  Request 1: GET /login
     -> the response contains <input name="csrf" value="AbC123...">
  Request 2: POST /login  (username, password, csrf)
     csrf:     Derive from prior response  (from the response of request 1)
     username: Use preset value  =  test@corp.local
     password: Use preset value  =  P@ssw0rd!
     Cookies:  add received cookies to jar = ON
```

Now on each playback the macro first grabs a fresh CSRF from the login page, then logs in with it - and puts the session cookies into the jar.

## Testing

**Test macro** plays the requests one by one, tries to derive parameter values, and shows: all requests and responses, the derived parameter values, the received cookies. This is the place where you make sure the token is really being extracted and the session comes up.

> Pitfall:
> - If the token is **single-use** (changes on every request), make sure the macro pulls it from the IMMEDIATELY preceding response, not from a cached preset value.
> - **Re-analyze macro** is handy to trigger after manual edits - Burp will rebuild the relationships between requests.
> - Extra requests in the macro (analytics, static) only slow down auto-login - keep in the chain only what is really needed to obtain the session.

entries.title: Network -> Connections: upstream/SOCKS proxy, platform auth, timeouts
entries.body:
# Network - Connections - how Burp goes out to the network

**Settings > Network > Connections** controls how Burp establishes outgoing connections: through which upstream proxy, through SOCKS, with what HTTP authentication to the server, and with what timeouts. Four blocks.

## Platform authentication

Automatic authentication of Burp to the target server (when the server itself requires HTTP authentication, rather than the application via a form).

- The **Do platform authentication** checkbox.
- **Add/Edit/Remove** - credentials per host. For each entry:
  - **Destination host** - the target host;
  - **Type** - `Basic`, `NTLMv1`, or `NTLMv2`;
  - **Username**, **Password**;
  - **Domain**, **Domain hostname** (for NTLM).
- **Prompt for credentials on platform authentication failure** - a pop-up login/password request on failure.

Applied at the user or project level (the **Override options for this project only** checkbox).

> Practice: if the target responds with `401 WWW-Authenticate: Basic/NTLM`, register the credentials here - and Repeater/Scanner will go out already authenticated, without having to glue an `Authorization` header onto every request by hand.

## Upstream proxy servers

Routing outgoing requests through an upstream proxy by rules based on the target host. The **first matching** rule from the top fires.

- **Destination host** - a mask (`*` - any characters, `?` - any character except a dot).
- **Proxy host** and **Proxy port**.
- **Authentication type** - `Basic`/`NTLMv1`/`NTLMv2` (plus Username/Password fields, and for NTLM - Domain, Domain hostname, SPNEGO, Negotiate).
- **Edit / Up / Down / Remove** - order matters.

```
Destination host   Proxy host        Port
*.internal.corp    proxy.corp.local  8080   (via the corporate proxy)
*                  (direct)          -      (everything else directly)
```

> Scenario: the target is reachable only from the corporate network through a proxy - you set up a rule for its domain. Or you chain Burp behind another proxy/anonymizer. To route traffic past the upstream, keep a `*` rule with no proxy last.

## SOCKS proxy

Works at the TCP level - ALL of Burp's outgoing traffic is wrapped into a single SOCKS proxy.

- **Use SOCKS proxy** + **Configure**: SOCKS host, SOCKS port, Username/Password (optional).
- **Do DNS lookups over SOCKS proxy** - resolve DNS on the proxy side (local DNS is disabled; important for working through Tor - fixes DNS leaks).
- **Hosts to bypass SOCKS proxying** - a list of exceptions (Add/Paste/Load/Remove/Clear).

> Practice: the classic "Burp -> Tor" combo is done here - SOCKS on `127.0.0.1:9050` and enable DNS over SOCKS. Note: SOCKS is global and hits all traffic, unlike per-host upstream rules.

## Timeouts

Everything is in seconds; `0` or empty = no timeout. Project-level.

- **Connect** - how long to wait for a connection to be established before considering the server unreachable.
- **Normal** - the ordinary network exchange timeout.
- **Open-ended responses** - for responses without `Content-Length`/`Transfer-Encoding`.
- **Domain name resolution** - how often to re-resolve a successfully resolved name.
- **Failed domain name resolution** - how often to retry a failed resolution.

> Pitfall: on slow/laggy targets during large Intruder attacks, a small **Connect** timeout produces an avalanche of false "timeout" responses. If you see drops - raise the timeouts.

entries.title: Network -> DNS: host overrides and IP version
entries.body:
# Network - DNS - controlling name resolution

**Settings > Network > DNS** lets Burp resolve hosts differently from the operating system. Two blocks, both project-level settings.

## Preferred IP version

Which IP version to prefer when resolving:

- **Use system's default behavior** - whatever the OS decides;
- **Prefer IPv4** - try IPv4 addresses first;
- **Prefer IPv6** - try IPv6 first.

> Practice: if a target has IPv4 and IPv6 configured differently (and that happens - the front end behind a CDN over IPv4, but the "bare" origin sticking out over IPv6, or vice versa), switching the version sometimes gives access to a different backend. Useful too when the network only allows one of the protocols.

## Hostname resolution overrides

The key thing in this section is a manual mapping of **host name -> a specific IP**, bypassing ordinary DNS. This is an analog of the `hosts` file, but local inside Burp.

Each entry: **Host** + **IP address**, with an enable checkbox and **Edit/Remove** buttons.

```
Host                       IP address
api.example.com            10.0.0.42      (hit past the CDN - straight to the origin)
admin.internal.example     127.0.0.1
staging.example.com        203.0.113.10
```

> Why this matters to a hunter:
> - **Bypassing CDN/WAF**: you found the real IP of the origin server (via DNS history, certificates, SSRF) - you register `target.com -> origin_IP` and send requests straight to the backend, bypassing Cloudflare/Akamai. The WAF does not see the traffic.
> - **Testing by vhost**: the same IP serves different sites based on the `Host` header. With an override you fix which exact IP to send to, while keeping the desired `Host`.
> - **When you have edited the system hosts file** for a non-proxy-aware client (invisible proxy) - an override guarantees that Burp itself forwards the request to the real address, instead of looping back on `127.0.0.1`.

## DNS cache

There is a button to flush Burp's DNS cache (**Clear cache** / flush). Trigger it after changing overrides or when the target's IP has changed - otherwise Burp will keep going to the cached old address.

> Note: this page has no DNS-over-HTTPS - it is specifically about resolution override and IP version preference.

entries.title: Network -> TLS: client certificates, protocols, SNI
entries.body:
# Network - TLS - fine-tuning TLS to the server

**Settings > Network > TLS** controls how Burp negotiates TLS with the target server (this is NOT about the proxy CA certificate for the browser - that is configured in Proxy). Four blocks.

## TLS negotiation

A choice of protocols and ciphers for outgoing connections to the server:

- **Verify upstream TLS** - verify the server's certificate (by default usually off for testing, so Burp does not break on the target's self-signed certificates).
- a set of protocols/ciphers: all those supported by Java / Java's defaults / **Use custom protocols and ciphers** (your own list).
- **Allow unsafe renegotiation** - permit unsafe renegotiation (turn on if the target breaks the handshake because of this).
- **Disable TLS session resume** - disable reuse of TLS sessions (caching).

> Practice: old/broken servers (legacy, embedded, banking software) often require outdated protocols or specific ciphers. If the connection does not come up - you switch to **custom** and enable the needed set; sometimes **Allow unsafe renegotiation** saves the day.

## Client TLS certificates

Certificates that Burp presents to the server when it requires **client certificate authentication** (mutual TLS, mTLS). This cures the situation "the site opens in an ordinary browser, but breaks through Burp".

- **Destination host** - a host mask (`*` - any host, `?` - one character except a dot).
- Formats: **PKCS#12** (`.p12`/`.pfx` with a password) or **PKCS#11** (hardware tokens/smart cards with a PIN).
- On a client certificate request, Burp takes the **first** host-matching certificate from the list.
- User or project level.

```
Destination host        Type      File
portal.bank.example     PKCS#12   client.p12   (password: ...)
*.gov.internal          PKCS#11   (smart card, PIN)
```

> Pitfall: if the target returns a `400`/drops the connection specifically through Burp, while working directly - it almost certainly requires a client certificate. You load a `.p12` here - and Repeater/Scanner start passing mTLS.

## Server TLS certificates

For viewing only: X509 certificates received from servers, with the ability to expand the details. Handy to glance at the SAN, the issuer, the validity period - sometimes internal host names stick out in the certificate (a lead for reconnaissance).

## Java TLS settings (require restarting Burp)

- **Enable algorithms blocked by Java security policy** - permit blocked/outdated algorithms (MD2 and the like) for truly ancient servers.
- **Disable Java SNI extension** - disable SNI on the Java side; fixes the handshake with broken servers that break on SNI.

> Related: a targeted **Override SNI** on an individual request is available in **Repeater** - useful for attacks on vhost/routing, when you need to send one SNI value while setting the `Host` header to a different one.

entries.title: Network -> HTTP: redirects, streaming, HTTP/2, keep-alive
entries.body:
# Network - HTTP - behavior at the HTTP protocol level

**Settings > Network > HTTP** controls how Burp handles redirects, streaming responses, HTTP versions, and service responses. Project-level.

## Redirections - which redirects Burp follows automatically

Checkboxes for which kinds of redirects to handle (relevant for Scanner/Intruder, where "follow redirects" is enabled):

- **3xx status code with Location header** - an ordinary HTTP redirect;
- **Refresh header** - a redirect via the `Refresh` HTTP header;
- **Meta refresh tag** - via `<meta http-equiv=refresh>` in HTML;
- **JavaScript-driven** - redirects initiated by JS;
- **Any status code with Location header** - follow `Location` on any code.

> Practice: in an automated attack it is often useful to let Burp **follow redirects by itself**, to reach the final page. But when testing open redirect - on the contrary, disable following, otherwise Burp will "swallow" the redirect itself and you will not see the raw `Location` with your payload domain.

## Streaming responses

For URLs that serve a continuous stream (AI chats, server-sent events, live updates). **Add/Edit** rules by URL mask and the options:

- **Store streaming responses** - save the full stream in Proxy history and Repeater (may bloat temp files);
- **Strip chunked encoding metadata** - remove chunked markers for readability (may break the client);
- **Treat responses with text/event-stream MIME type as streaming** - automatically treat SSE as streaming.

The proxy serves the stream to the client in real time; the other tools close the connection with such a response.

## Status 100 Continue

- **Understand 100 Continue responses** - correctly skip the intermediate `100 Continue` response;
- **Remove 100 Continue headers** - strip these intermediate headers.

## HTTP/1

- **Use keep-alive for HTTP/1 if the server supports it** - reuse the TCP connection for several request/response pairs (Burp closes an idle connection after 5 seconds).

## HTTP/2

- **Default to HTTP/2 if the server supports it** - use HTTP/2 if the server announces support in the TLS handshake (ALPN). Can be overridden per tool via the Inspector or a specific tool's settings.

> Why a hunter wants HTTP/2: many **request smuggling** and **HTTP/2 downgrade** attacks require deliberate control of the version. When testing smuggling - switch HTTP/1 <-> HTTP/2 by hand (Repeater has a version choice and a "send as is" mode), rather than relying on auto.

entries.title: UI -> Side panel: side panel position and Inspector
entries.body:
# User interface - Side panel - the side panel

**Settings > User interface > Side panel** configures the side panel that appears in all tools (except Intruder - it has its own layout settings). On this panel live the **Inspector** and related widgets - a breakdown of the request/response into components (parameters, headers, cookies), editing on the fly.

These are **user-level settings** - they apply across all Burp installations on the machine.

## Default side panel layout

- **Position** - where to keep the panel: **Left** or **Right**.
- Display mode:
  - **Auto-expand** - the panel expands/collapses itself depending on the screen space;
  - **Always collapsed** - keep it collapsed by default (you expand it with a click when needed).

## Inspector widgets

Configuration of individual Inspector widgets:

- **Show** - show the widget or hide it;
- **Open by default** - expand the widget automatically when the Inspector opens;
- **Wrap text** - wrap text inside the widget's cells;
- **Up / Down** - the widget order in the list.

```
Widget                 Show  Open by default  Wrap
Request attributes      [x]        [ ]         [ ]
Request query params    [x]        [x]         [ ]
Request headers         [x]        [ ]         [x]
Response headers        [x]        [ ]         [x]
```

> Practice: move to the top and expand by default the widgets you touch most often - usually those are **query/body params** and **headers**. Then in Repeater you immediately see the parsed parameters, and changing a token/ID value is faster than hunting for it by eye in the raw request.

> Tip: on a small monitor set **Always collapsed** + **Right**, so the panel does not eat the width of the editor window, and expand the Inspector selectively for a specific request.

entries.title: UI -> Message editor: font, highlighting, pretty print
entries.body:
# User interface - Message editor - how HTTP messages look

**Settings > User interface > Message editor** controls the display of HTTP requests and responses in ALL of Burp's editors (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder, etc.). Separate from **Display settings**, which is only responsible for the menu font.

## Font

- A choice of typeface and font size specifically for the body of HTTP messages.
- **Font smoothing** - font anti-aliasing.

> Practice: set a monospaced font (Consolas, JetBrains Mono, Fira Code) - in it you clearly see alignment, offsets, and suspicious invisible characters in payloads; in a proportional font this is lost.

## Syntax highlighting

- **Highlight request syntax** - syntax highlighting in requests (JavaScript, JSON, CSS, etc.);
- **Highlight response syntax** - the same for responses.

## Pretty print

- **Pretty print by default** - automatically "tidy up" data/markup/code: standard line breaks and indentation. JSON, XML, HTML, CSS, JavaScript are supported.

> Practice: **Pretty print** is indispensable for bloated JSON responses and minified JS bundles - the structure becomes readable, it is easier to pick out hidden endpoints, keys, flags. In the editor Burp switches between the **Pretty / Raw / Hex** views - the raw view is needed when every byte matters (for example, during smuggling you count `Content-Length` by hand).

## Hide decoded text on hover

- **Hide decoded text on hover** - disable the tooltip with the decoded text on hover over an encoded value (by default Burp shows the URL/HTML-encoding decoding in a tooltip).

## Character set

Four encoding-handling options:

- **Recognize automatically** - from the message headers;
- **Platform default** (UTF-8);
- **Raw bytes** (ASCII) - show the raw bytes;
- a specific set selected manually.

> Pitfall: when working with non-Latin scripts (Cyrillic, CJK) and with binary payloads the encoding mode matters - the wrong character set turns the data into "mojibake" and throws off the length. For binary stuff switch to **Raw bytes / Hex**.

entries.title: UI -> Hotkeys: keyboard shortcuts and their customization
entries.body:
# User interface - Hotkeys - keyboard shortcuts

**Settings > User interface > Hotkeys** configures key combinations for frequent commands. Mastering hotkeys is, without exaggeration, the main speed boost for working in Burp: less mouse, a faster "found a request -> sent it to Repeater -> iterated" loop.

## How to remap

1. **Settings > User interface > Hotkeys**.
2. Double-click the command in the list.
3. Press the desired combination -> apply.

Limitations: **any hotkey must include `Ctrl`/`Cmd`** (you may add `Shift`). On Windows, when editing text avoid `Ctrl+Alt` - the system may take it for `AltGr`.

## Default hotkeys worth knowing by heart

**Sending a request between tools:**
```
Ctrl + R          Send to Repeater
Ctrl + I          Send to Intruder
Ctrl + Shift + R  switch to the Repeater tab
Ctrl + Shift + I  switch to the Intruder tab
```

**Proxy / Intercept:**
```
Ctrl + F          Forward (release the intercepted request)
Ctrl + T          Intercept on/off (the intercept toggle)
```

**Repeater:**
```
Ctrl + Space      Send request
```

**Encoding/decoding the selection (right in the editor):**
```
Ctrl + B          Base64-encode the selection
Ctrl + Shift + B  Base64-decode
Ctrl + U          URL-encode
Ctrl + Shift + U  URL-decode
Ctrl + H          replace the selection with HTML entities
```

**Navigation across the main tabs:**
```
Ctrl + Shift + D  Dashboard
Ctrl + Shift + T  Target
Ctrl + Shift + P  Proxy
```

> Practice: the working minimum that speeds up everything is `Ctrl+R` (in Repeater), `Ctrl+Space` (send), `Ctrl+Shift+R` (go to Repeater), and `Ctrl+U`/`Ctrl+Shift+U` (quickly URL-encode a payload right in the selection). The encoding hotkeys especially help with manual payload editing - you select a chunk, `Ctrl+U`, and do not go into the Decoder.

> Note: the specific defaults may differ slightly between Burp versions; the exact current list is always visible right in the table on this settings page.

entries.title: UI -> Display: theme, menu font, scaling
entries.body:
# User interface - Display - the look of the interface

**Settings > User interface > Display** is responsible for the overall look of the Burp window: the theme, the MENU font, and scaling. These are **user-level settings** (for all installations on the machine).

> Important: these settings do NOT control the font of HTTP messages - that is configured separately in **Message editor**.

## Appearance

- Theme: **light** or **dark**.
- The size of Burp's menu font.

## Scaling (for HiDPI/4K)

- **Use system DPI settings** - scale automatically by the system and display settings;
- or manually pick a specific scaling factor.

Changes require restarting Burp.

> Practice: on a 4K monitor Burp is often too small by default - pick a manual scale (125-150%). If the interface is "blurry" or elements overlap - play between auto-DPI and a manual value.

## Other

- **Merge top-level menu with system title bar** (Linux/Windows, on by default) - embed the main menu into the system title bar, saving vertical space. On macOS the option is absent.
- **Zebra stripe** / without it - alternating row highlighting in tables (history, results) for readability.
- Whether to show the **Learn** tab in the main panel (tutorials and learning material).

> Tip: a dark theme + zebra stripe noticeably reduce eye strain over long sessions, when you scroll through HTTP history for hours.

entries.title: Suite -> REST API: automation and launching scans from outside
entries.body:
# Suite - REST API - controlling Burp over HTTP

**Settings > Suite > REST API** enables the built-in REST service through which Burp can be driven programmatically - first and foremost to **launch scans from CI/CD, scripts, and your own tooling**, without touching the GUI.

## Enabling

- The **Service running** checkbox - bring the service up.
- **Change** next to the current URL - the configuration dialog: the bind port + address (loopback / all interfaces / a specific one) -> **OK**.

> Security: PortSwigger strongly recommends against binding to non-loopback interfaces on an untrusted network. The REST API gives powerful capabilities (up to launching scans) - by opening it outward you effectively hand over control of Burp.

## API keys

By default access requires a key:

- **New** - create a key: set a name -> **Copy key to clipboard** and save it (the key is shown ONLY at creation);
- **Edit** / **Delete** - rename/delete;
- **Allow access without API key** - disable authorization (NOT recommended: it opens the door to CSRF attacks on Burp itself).

## Documentation and endpoints

After launch the interactive documentation and the calls themselves are available at:

```
[Service URL]/[API key]
# for example:  http://127.0.0.1:1337/<your-key>
```

The main thing this is needed for is the programmatic launch of scanning and retrieval of results:

```bash
# Launch a scan of the specified URL via the REST API
curl -X POST 'http://127.0.0.1:1337/<API-KEY>/v0.1/scan' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"urls":["https://target.example.com"]}'
# the response returns a task_id; the status and issues are pulled separately via a GET by this id
```

> Practice: the REST API is the way to embed Burp scans into a pipeline (DAST on every release) or to run a series of targets via a script. For each client/script create a SEPARATE key - that makes it easier to revoke access and to understand who ran what.

> Note: full automation (managing scope, configs, exporting reports) is the realm of Burp Suite Professional/Enterprise; the set of endpoints reflects exactly that.

entries.title: Suite -> Updates: auto-updates and release channels
entries.body:
# Suite - Updates - Burp updates

**Settings > Suite > Updates** controls how Burp updates itself. These are installation-level settings (not account-level) - which is why Stable and Early Adopter can be kept installed and run in parallel.

## Auto-updates

- **Enable auto-updates** - automatically download available updates. When a new version is ready, Burp offers to restart; **Later** postpones the installation until the next launch.

> Limitation: auto-update works only if Burp is launched as an executable (the native launcher). When launched **from a JAR** auto-update is unavailable - you download versions manually from the PortSwigger site.

## Release channels

Two channels:

- **Stable** (default) - fully tested releases;
- **Early Adopter** - new features and improvements land here FIRST, before the general release.

```
Channel:
  ( ) Stable          <- stable, for production/client work
  (•) Early Adopter   <- fresh features before everyone, but a risk of rough edges
```

Nuances:

- if you manually download a version available only in Early Adopter, the channel automatically switches to Early Adopter;
- rolling back to an older version may lead to incompatibility with recent project files.

> Practice: for bug bounty and research **Early Adopter** is convenient - new techniques (especially in the Scanner and the HTTP engine) arrive earlier. For contract/client work, where predictability matters, keep **Stable**. Since the channels are separate installations, you can have both and switch by task.

> Pitfall: after a major update the proxy CA certificate or the engine behavior sometimes changes - if HTTPS "suddenly broke" or extensions fell off, check whether there was just an update.

entries.title: Suite -> Performance feedback: telemetry and error logs
entries.body:
# Suite - Performance feedback - feedback and memory

**Settings > Suite > Performance feedback** is responsible for voluntarily sending technical information about Burp's operation to PortSwigger and for local error logging. Also here (in the same settings area) the maximum memory is set. User-level settings.

## Performance feedback (telemetry)

- The **Automatically submit feedback about Burp's performance** checkbox - voluntarily send technical information about Burp's internal operation. The data is tied to a debug ID, which identifies the session where an exception occurred.
- **Anonymization:** the stack trace inside the logged exceptions is fully obfuscated - personal data cannot be read out of the file.

> Practice: for private engagements and work with sensitive targets I keep telemetry **off** - extra outbound traffic from the tool is not needed, even obfuscated.

## Log exceptions to a local directory

- For environments where external data transfer is forbidden, instead of sending you can **write errors locally**: the file gets a timestamp, a short description, and an obfuscated stack trace.

> Why: if Burp periodically misbehaves (extensions crash, strange drops) - you enable the local exception log and then dissect what exactly is failing, without giving anything outward.

## Maximum memory usage

- The maximum memory for Burp; by default ~50% of the system RAM (minimum 256 MB).

> Practice: large scans and a bloated HTTP history eat memory. On a fat target raise the limit (if the RAM allows) - otherwise Burp will start to lag and dump data into temp files. But do not give Burp all the memory: the OS and the browser need some left too.

entries.title: Suite -> Temporary files location: where Burp keeps temporary files
entries.body:
# Suite - Temporary files location - placement of temporary files

**Settings > Suite > Temporary files location** sets where Burp stores temporary working files. This is where data that does not fit in memory goes: response bodies, history, intermediate scan data. A user-level setting.

## Behavior

- By default Burp creates a directory in the OS's standard temp location - by itself, with no user involvement.
- If needed you can specify **your own directory**. A quote from the docs: you may want a different volume or a "location that is not externally readable".

```
Temporary files location:
  (•) Use the default system location
  ( ) Use a custom location:  D:\burp-tmp   (your own volume / encrypted partition)
```

Changes take effect after restarting Burp.

## Why change it

- **Disk space.** Burp's temp files on a large scan can easily grow to tens of gigabytes. If the system disk (where temp usually sits) is small - move temp to a spacious volume, otherwise Burp will hit "out of space" and crash mid-scan.
- **Security/privacy.** Raw requests and responses of the target settle in the temporary files - potentially sensitive data. On a shared/foreign machine it is reasonable to keep temp on an encrypted volume or one others cannot access.
- **Performance.** Temp on a fast SSD noticeably speeds up working with a heavy history.
- **macOS nuance:** the system sometimes cleans the default temp after hibernation - specifying your own directory cures this.

> Pitfall: do not put temp on a network drive or a slow HDD - on large scans Burp will lag wildly because of constant access to temp. And remember: with a temporary project everything valuable lives precisely in temp and is lost when Burp closes; for long-term work use a disk-based project (Pro).

entries.title: Suite -> Startup behavior: pausing tasks and trusting the project
entries.body:
# Suite - Startup behavior - behavior at startup

**Settings > Suite > Startup behavior** controls what Burp does when opening a project - with an emphasis on the safety of opening foreign/untrusted project files. User-level settings.

## Pause Automated Tasks (on startup)

- When enabled, Burp on opening a project does NOT let automated tasks immediately send requests - all automated tasks start paused.

> Why: you open a project file received from someone (a colleague, a client, off the internet). It may have saved active scan tasks with a defined scope. Without the pause Burp on opening will instantly start hammering those targets - and you may not have permission for them and may not even be on the right network. The pause lets you look around before anything goes out.

## Trust this project

- Uncheck **Trust this project** if you want Burp to wipe potentially dangerous settings out of the project BEFORE opening it. This also prevents automated tasks from running in the untrusted scenario.
- If trust is not confirmed, automated tasks remain paused by default - as additional protection.

```
Startup behavior:
  [x] Pause Automated Tasks on startup
  [ ] Trust this project   <- unchecked for foreign/unverified .burp files
```

> An important warning from the docs: even with settings wiped, PortSwigger does NOT recommend proxying traffic through an untrusted project file. A project file can carry malicious configuration (for example, match-and-replace, extensions, an upstream proxy) that will redirect or tamper with your traffic.

> Hunter practice: treat any project file not from your own hands as untrusted - open it with **Trust** unchecked and tasks paused, inspect the scope, match/replace, the extension list, and the upstream proxy, and only then launch anything manually.

entries.title: Suite -> Shutdown behavior: confirmation on close
entries.body:
# Suite - Shutdown behavior - behavior on close

**Settings > Suite > Shutdown behavior** is a short section about how Burp behaves on exit. A user-level setting (for all installations on the machine).

## Shutdown confirmation

- By default, on close Burp shows a confirmation dialog ("exit for sure?").
- **Don't confirm before closing Burp** - remove this confirmation and close immediately.

```
Shutdown behavior:
  [ ] Don't confirm before closing Burp
      <- leave it OFF, so you do not close Burp by accident mid-scan
```

> Practice: keep the confirmation **on** (that is, do NOT tick "Don't confirm"). It is very easy out of habit to close the window with `Ctrl+W`/the cross in the middle of an active scan or with an unsaved temporary project - and lose all the history and work. An extra "yes" click is cheaper than a lost session.

> Note: the fate of unfinished tasks and the saving of the project depend on the project type - a disk-based project (Pro) writes data to disk as it goes and survives a close; a temporary project keeps everything in memory/temp and loses data on exit. So for long work it matters more not the confirmation itself, but that you work in a project that is saved to disk.

entries.title: Extensions: Java/Python/Ruby environments and auto-loading
entries.body:
# Extensions - extension settings

**Settings > Extensions** configures the runtime environment of Burp extensions and their auto-loading. The extensions themselves are installed and managed on the **Extensions** tab (BApp Store + manual loading), and here - the environment and behavior at startup.

## Structure

- **Core extension settings** - the built-in extension management framework.
- Panels added by specific installed extensions.

## Behavior at startup

- **Reload extensions on startup** - reload all extensions when Burp launches (can be disabled with the command-line flag `--disable-extensions`).
- **Automatically update installed BApps on startup** - auto-check for extension updates from the BApp Store.
- **Hide dialog when reloading** - by default, on reload the **Load Burp extension** dialog appears; uncheck this to see the reload details.

## Runtime environments

Burp is written in Java; to load extensions written in Python and Ruby you need bridges:

**Java:**
- the folder where Burp looks for JAR files to add to the extension loader's classpath.

**Python (via Jython):**
- the path to the standalone Jython JAR;
- **Folder for loading modules** - the directory added to Python's `sys.path` (so extensions can see their modules/dependencies).

```
Python environment:
  Location of Jython standalone JAR file:  C:\tools\jython-standalone-2.7.3.jar
  Folder for loading modules:              C:\tools\python-libs
```

**Ruby (via JRuby):**
- the path to the standalone JRuby JAR for Ruby extensions.

> Practice: a mass of useful BApps are written in Python (Jython) - without the specified path to jython-standalone they simply will not load, and the log will have an error about a missing environment. Download the jython-standalone JAR, register it here - and the Python extensions will work. Likewise JRuby for Ruby.

> Pitfall: the Python and Ruby environments, when loading lots of extensions, can eat memory and hit the limits - if Burp started crashing after installing a heap of Python BApps, check the maximum memory (see Performance) and do not keep extra ones enabled. View the extension's logs and errors on the extension's own tab (Output/Errors).

entries.title: Configuration library: reusable sets of scan settings
entries.body:
# Configuration library

**Settings > Configuration library** stores ready-made sets of settings for specific tasks - usually for **crawl** or **audit**. Instead of configuring a scan manually each time, you pick a named configuration (or several) and apply it when launching a task.

## What is inside

- **Built-in** - built-in configurations for typical scenarios (crawl profiles and sets of Scanner audit checks: "XSS only", "lightweight crawl", "audit checks - critical issues only", and the like).
- **Custom** - your own.

A filter in the interface separates **Built-in** and **Custom**; there is **Edit / Duplicate / Delete**.

## Creating your own configuration

1. **Add** -> choose the type (function) of the configuration.
2. Set a unique name.
3. Tweak the needed settings.

Important: you do not have to configure everything. If a configuration does not define some area - for it the defaults or current values are taken.

## Applying and layering

You can load **several configurations at once**. They are applied in order, and the lower one overrides the upper one where they touch the same settings.

```
Example of a combination when launching an audit:
  1. "Crawl - fast"                 (base: how to crawl)
  2. "Audit - XSS only"             (which checks to run)
  3. "My - throttle & headers"      (own timeouts/headers - will override the upper ones)
```

## Import / Export (JSON)

Configurations are exported and imported as human-readable **JSON** - for backup, sharing between installations, and for launching from the command line. Import only JSON from trusted sources.

> Bug bounty practice: assemble your own profiles for recurring tasks - "a fast passive pass over a new target", "an aggressive audit only against a specific bug class", "a quiet mode with throttling so as not to knock over a frail server". Configure once, save to the library - and from then on launching a scan is a single selection, without manually fiddling with dozens of checkboxes.

> Connection with CLI/Enterprise: these same JSON configs are fed to Burp when launched from the command line and are used in Burp Suite Enterprise - that is, a profile fine-tuned in the desktop carries over into the automated pipeline.

entries.title: Response extraction rules: pulling tokens out of responses
entries.body:
# Response extraction rules

This is the mechanism by which Burp pulls a specific value out of an HTTP response, even if the surrounding response text is different every time. The rule describes once "where the needed value sits", and from then on Burp applies it to any responses.

It is used in three places:

- **Macros / session handling** - find the value of a custom parameter (for example, a fresh CSRF token) in a response, to insert it into the next request. This is a key building block of auto-login.
- **Intruder** - grep-extract: pull data out of attack responses (output the value into a results column).
- **Sequencer** - specify where a custom session token sits for randomness analysis.

## Method 1. Define start and end (by boundaries)

The value is defined by "before" and "after" markers:

**Start:**
- **Start after expression** - the literal text BEFORE the needed value;
- **Start at offset** - a fixed offset in bytes.

**End:**
- **End at delimiter** - the literal text AFTER the value;
- **End at fixed length** - take exactly N bytes from the start.

Escape sequences are supported: `\r`, `\n`, `\xNN` (hex ASCII), `\\`.

## Method 2. Define from regex group

You specify a regular expression with a group - the group's contents are extracted.

```
<title>(.*?)</title>        -> the page title text
(\d\d\d\d\d\d)               -> a six-digit number (for example, an OTP)
```

## Extracting a CSRF token - two ways

Suppose the response has: `<input name="csrf_token" value="AbC123XyZ">`.

**By boundaries:**
```
Start after expression:  name="csrf_token" value="
End at delimiter:        "
-> extracts:  AbC123XyZ
```

**By regex (one line):**
```
name="csrf_token" value="([^"]+)"
-> group 1 = AbC123XyZ
```

## Verification

During configuration Burp **highlights right in the response** the fragment that will be extracted - so you immediately see whether you hit the target or not. The **Refetch response** button pulls a fresh response, so you can run the rule against different responses and make sure it reliably catches the token (rather than latching onto a random match).

> Practice: for CSRF/anti-forgery tokens a **regex anchored by the field name** (`name="csrf..." value="([^"]+)"`) is more reliable than "start at offset" by a fixed position - the markup shifts, and a rigid offset will break on the very first change to the page. The chain "response extraction rule in a macro -> derive parameter" is what makes auto-login correctly pass CSRF protection during scans.
